policy_statement: As part of domestic energy sector reforms, Saudi Arabia aims to increase the utilization of natural gas in its energy mix, with up to 50% of electricity generation expected to be met by natural gas by 2030.
mechanism_description: The Kingdom aims to increase natural gas use in electricity generation to displace more carbon-intensive fuels and lower the carbon intensity of domestic power generation.
primary_causal_pathway: Electricity generation fuel mix shift → ↓ CO₂e per kWh (fuel combustion + upstream methane) → ↓ power-sector GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Utilities and system operators increase natural-gas-fired electricity generation (and associated gas supply infrastructure/dispatch) to replace electricity previously generated from more carbon-intensive fuels (commonly oil or coal).  
2) Mediating physical process: Natural gas combustion emits less CO₂ per unit energy than coal/oil and can increase thermal efficiency in modern combined-cycle plants; net climate impact also depends on upstream methane leakage during extraction, processing, and transport.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower average grid emissions intensity (CO₂e/kWh) and reduced total power-sector GHG emissions relative to the displaced generation (potentially partially offset if methane leakage is high or if it delays zero-carbon deployment).
dominant_pathway_test: Fuel switching in power plants from higher-carbon fuels to natural gas most strongly reduces lifecycle GHG emissions per unit electricity, lowering overall power-sector emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Fuel substitution in electricity generation changes combustion emissions intensity, yielding lower CO₂e per kWh and thus lowering total GHG emissions—this is a mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While gas switching can affect resource efficiency (higher efficiency per kWh) and reliability, the core climate-relevant intent and effect is reduced GHG emissions versus higher-carbon fuels, not reduced exposure to hazards (adaptation) or ecosystem-mediated benefits (nature-based).  
3) Edge cases applied: If gas displaces renewables/nuclear, emissions could rise; if methane leakage is very high, net benefit could be small or negative; if framed mainly as energy security, the climate effect still hinges on emissions intensity. These do not change the mechanism’s primary mitigation orientation for the class “gas displaces more carbon-intensive power.”  
4) Applicability to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that increases natural gas generation specifically to displace more carbon-intensive electricity sources shares the same primary mitigation pathway of lowering grid carbon intensity via fuel switching.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether the policy is best tagged as “fuel switching,” “efficiency/CCGT upgrades,” “methane control,” or “transition/bridge fuel” is policy-evidence-specific and should be assigned at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Net mitigation depends on what generation is displaced and on upstream methane leakage; potential lock-in/stranded-asset risk and delayed renewable deployment can reduce long-run mitigation benefits, but the dominant intended mechanism remains emissions reduction via lower-carbon fuel substitution.
key_indicators: "aims to increase the utilization of natural gas in its energy mix"; "By 2030"; "up to 50% of its electricity generation"; "significantly lowering the carbon intensity"
co_benefits: Potential air-quality improvements (lower SOx/PM than oil/coal), energy system efficiency/reliability, domestic fuel-cost savings; not explicitly stated.
instance_edge_case_notes: Framed as part of “reforms” but no concrete policy lever (e.g., mandate, subsidy reform, plant buildout) is specified; classification therefore rests on the explicit quantitative mix target (50% by 2030). Methane leakage is not mentioned, which could affect net mitigation outcome but not the instrument classification.
